I heartily disagree , and as an STSer and an avid constructionist generally , I find Hunt , et al , excellent , probably the best allies we can have .Hunt et al defend our flanks very nicely against attacks from the Gross and Leavitt types precisely because they keep us from falling into the purely idealist / relativist traps that lit - crit po-mos seem to .

They view " external reality " ( for lack of a better term -- the use is mine alone , not Hunt 's ) not as a necessary evil to which we must make concessions , but as an active referent to keep our speculations well grounded .

context: I heartily disagree , and as an STSer and an avid constructionist generally , I find Hunt , et al , excellent , probably the best allies we can have .

Hunt et al defend our flanks very nicely against attacks from the Gross and Leavitt types precisely because they keep us from falling into the purely idealist / relativist traps that lit - crit po-mos seem to .They view " external reality " ( for lack of a better term -- the use is mine alone , not Hunt 's ) not as a necessary evil to which we must make concessions , but as an active referent to keep our speculations well grounded .

The position that Hunt et al take reminds me a bit of Rorty , in that they recognize the validity of po-mo and constructivist criticisms , but worry about the ability to say anything at all if we take those positions to their logical extents .

They view " external reality " ( for lack of a better term -- the use is mine alone , not Hunt 's ) not as a necessary evil to which we must make concessions , but as an active referent to keep our speculations well grounded .The position that Hunt et al take reminds me a bit of Rorty , in that they recognize the validity of po-mo and constructivist criticisms , but worry about the ability to say anything at all if we take those positions to their logical extents .

Not unlike Latour 's notion of speed bumps ( that American can call them spped bumps , Brits can call them sleeping policemen , but if you hit them too fast , regardless of what they 're called , they 'll bang the hell out of your car ) , Hunt et al and Rorty 's observations give us practical ways to bring reality back in .

While some of our STS allies might consider such an approach to be a sellout ( in that it places historians back in the center as arbiters of " truth " and believers in same ) , it does come to grips in some fashion with the idealism vs. realism / materialism problem .

Better still , our emerging use of a " representations vs realities " framework is quite powerful in explaining the formation of popular consciousness and ideology . In my own work , I can set constructions of housewifery , happy taylorized workers , and ideologies of progress , promulgated by modernizers , against the grim realities I find in the archives and set a nice dialectic into motion .

For some in this field , being ' politically astute ' means making oneself usable as a resource in many different contexts by not explicitly raising the political consequences of what one does -- or by saying that they will vary so much across contexts that there 's much one can do about it , except to fight specific political battles one cares about deeply .

A lot depends on what you mean by ' politically astute ' .For some in this field , being ' politically astute ' means making oneself usable as a resource in many different contexts by not explicitly raising the political consequences of what one does -- or by saying that they will vary so much across contexts that there 's much one can do about it , except to fight specific political battles one cares about deeply .

In both cases , politics gets taken out of the STS arena and situated somewhere else ( e.g. in your conscience ) . Ironically , when scientists were the ones doing a kind of science studies -- e.g. J.D. Bernal v. Michael Polanyi in Cold War UK -- the politics of science would be a major topic of discussion .

Michael Polanyi in Cold War UK -- the politics of science would be a major topic of discussion .As Stephen Turner reminded me off - net recently , it was the social scientists -- Merton in particular -- who tended to de-politicize the discussion of science as they were turning the natural sciences into an object of social inquiry .

Science studies has a tendency to veer in one of two directions .

On the one hand , there are those who do n't criticize scientists because they 're not talking to scientists but only to other ( virtual ? ) science studies practitioners .On the other hand , there are those who do n't criticize scientists because they basically see themselves as helping scientists accomplish their goals -- a bit like public relations people .

I see ' criticism ' as designed to avoid these two extremes , so as to maintain an engaged independence . Moreover , I find it a bit disappointing that in the past few years , several leaders in the field have tried to distance science studies from science criticism .
